mnemonics.title: CFG Components
mnemonics.content: Remember CFG = T + V + P + S: 'Tina Vomits Puking Stuff' - Terminals, Variables (Non-terminals), Productions, Start symbol

mnemonics.title: CNF Rule
mnemonics.content: CNF = 'Can't Make Exceptions': productions either A → BC or A → a (single terminal). No ε, no unit productions.

mnemonics.title: CFL Closure Properties
mnemonics.content: CFLs are 'UNION-CAT-Kleen' but NOT 'INTERSECT-COMPLEMENT': Closed under Union, Concatenation, Kleene Star. Not closed under Intersection or Complement.

mnemonics.title: GNF Definition
mnemonics.content: GNF = 'Go Nice & Fast': Left side always starts with a terminal (A → aα)

mnemonics.title: Ambiguity Detection
mnemonics.content: Ambiguous = 'Another Beast Might Invade Grammar' - if Multiple Parse Trees exist for same string

acronyms.usage: Formal grammar with productions A → α where A is non-terminal
Standard form: A → BC or A → a
Standard form: A → aα (starts with terminal)
Replacing leftmost non-terminal at each step
Replacing rightmost non-terminal at each step

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an upside-down family tree: Start symbol at root, branching down to terminals at leaves. Like a sentence diagram.
Think of derivation as 'find and replace' game: pick a non-terminal, swap it with RHS of a production rule.
Draw two different tree structures for the same string - like two ways to parenthesize 2+3*4 giving different results.
A snake eating its tail - A → A means the symbol refers back to itself on the left side, causing infinite loop in parsing.
Like a balloon you can inflate/deflate: split string into 5 parts (uvxyz), pump middle (vy) to get more strings in language.

analogies.analogy: Think of CFG like English grammar: Terminals = words, Non-terminals = grammatical categories (noun, verb), Productions = grammar rules (sentence → subject + predicate), Start symbol = 'sentence'.
Derivation is like cooking a recipe: start with 'dish' (S), apply production rules to transform ingredients until you get the final 'cake' (terminal string).
Parse tree is like an organizational chart: CEO at top (start symbol), managers below (non-terminals), workers at bottom (terminals).
Ambiguous grammar is like 'Time flies like an arrow' - can be parsed two ways giving different meanings (time as subject vs 'time flies' as verb phrase).
Regular languages can count (modulo), CFLs can match pairs (like nesting parentheses). Like comparing a basic calculator (regular) to one with parentheses (CFL).
Stack in PDA is like a plate stack in a cafeteria: push plates when opening (aⁿ), pop when closing (bⁿ) - must match in reverse order.
